questions.question: Which prominent Indian freedom fighter was imprisoned in Mandalay Jail, Burma?
questions.solution:
Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Mandalay Jail

Bal Gangadhar Tilak was a central figure in the Indian independence movement. He was imprisoned in Mandalay Jail, Burma (now Myanmar), from 1908 to 1914 for writing articles in his newspaper, Kesari, that were deemed seditious by the British government.

  • During his six years in exile, he wrote the famous book Gita Rahasya, an interpretation of the Bhagavad Gita.
  • He was a leader of the 'Extremist' faction of the Indian National Congress and famously declared, "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it!"

questions.question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the Indian National Congress (INC)? a) The INC was founded in 1885 by Allan Octavian Hume. b) The first session of the INC was held in Pune under the presidency of WC Bonnerjee.
questions.solution:
Founding of the Indian National Congress (INC)

Statement (a) is correct, while statement (b) is partially incorrect regarding the location.

  • Founding: The INC was indeed founded in December 1885 by Allan Octavian Hume (A.O. Hume), a retired British officer.
  • First Session: While W.C. Bonnerjee was the first president, the session was held in Bombay (Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College), not Pune. It was originally planned for Pune, but shifted due to a cholera outbreak.

questions.question: Who was honoured with the title 'Assam Kesari' for his courageous role in the Indian freedom movement?
questions.solution:
Assam Kesari: Ambikagiri Raichoudhury

Ambikagiri Raichoudhury (1885–1967) was a notable Indian freedom fighter, poet, and social reformer from Assam. He is popularly known as 'Assam Kesari' (The Lion of Assam) for his fiery nationalist spirit and contributions to Assamese literature and the independence struggle.

questions.question: Who is known as the 'Mother of Indian Revolution' for her pioneering role in the early Indian independence movement and for unfurling the first version of the Indian national flag abroad?
questions.solution:
Madam Bhikaji Cama

Madam Bhikaji Cama is known as the 'Mother of Indian Revolution'. She was a prominent revolutionary who worked extensively in Europe to garner support for Indian independence.

  • In 1907, she became the first person to unfurl the Indian National Flag on foreign soil during the International Socialist Conference in Stuttgart, Germany.
  • The flag she hoisted was a precursor to the modern Indian tricolor, featuring green, saffron, and red stripes.

questions.question: Who is popularly known as the 'Father of Indian Unrest' for his active role in the Indian independence movement?
questions.solution:
Father of Indian Unrest

British colonial authorities, specifically journalist Valentine Chirol, described Bal Gangadhar Tilak as the 'Father of Indian Unrest'. They viewed him as the primary instigator of the nationalist sentiment that challenged British rule through mass mobilization and radical rhetoric.

questions.question: Bhawani Pathak and Devi Choudhurani were famous leaders associated with which of the following?
questions.solution:
Fakir and Sanyasi Rebellion

Bhawani Pathak and Devi Choudhurani were iconic leaders of the Sanyasi and Fakir Rebellion (late 18th century) in Bengal. This was a series of revolts by monks (Sanyasis) and Muslim mendicants (Fakirs) against the East India Company's restrictive policies and high tax collections following the Great Famine of 1770.

questions.question: The first armed tribal rebellion against the British is considered to have taken place in:
questions.solution:
Tilka Manjhi and the First Tribal Revolt

The first armed tribal rebellion against the British is often attributed to Tilka Manjhi in 1784. He led the Paharia tribe in the Rajmahal Hills (present-day Jharkhand) against the British East India Company. He is remembered for attacking and killing Augustus Cleveland, the British Collector, with a poisoned arrow.

questions.question: The first parallel government, following the Quit India Movement, was established in which city?
questions.solution:
Parallel Governments in Quit India Movement

During the Quit India Movement (1942), several 'parallel governments' (Prati Sarkar) were established across India. While Ballia had the first short-lived one under Chittu Pandey, the Satara Parallel Government in Maharashtra (led by Nana Patil) was the most organized and longest-lasting, surviving until 1946.

questions.question: The Ghadar Party, established in 1913 in San Francisco, aimed to overthrow the British rule in India through revolutionary means. Who was the key founder and ideological leader of the Ghadar Party?
questions.solution:
The Ghadar Party

The Ghadar Party was an international political movement founded by expatriate Indians to overthrow British rule in India. Lala Har Dayal was the key founding member and the ideological driving force behind the party, serving as its General Secretary and editing the weekly journal Ghadar.

questions.question: The Home Rule Movement in India, launched during the World War I, aimed to attain self-government within the British Empire. Which two prominent leaders were associated with starting this movement?
questions.option_1: Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhas Chandra Bose
questions.option_2: Mahatma Gandhi and Gopal Krishna Gokhale
questions.option_3: Lala Lajpat Rai and Bipin Chandra Pal
questions.option_4: Annie Besant and Bal Gangadhar Tilak
questions.solution:
Home Rule Movement

The Home Rule Movement (1916–1918) was led by two separate leagues:

  • Bal Gangadhar Tilak: Launched his league in April 1916, operating mainly in Maharashtra and Karnataka.
  • Annie Besant: Launched her league in September 1916, covering the rest of India.

They aimed for 'Home Rule' or self-government for India within the British Empire, similar to the status enjoyed by Canada and Australia.

questions.question: Who was the leader of the 'Indian National Army' before Subhas Chandra Bose took charge?
questions.solution:
The Indian National Army (INA)

The Indian National Army was first conceived and organized by Captain Mohan Singh in 1942 with the help of Japanese officials in Southeast Asia. It was composed of Indian Prisoners of War (POWs). Subhas Chandra Bose took over the leadership and revitalized the INA (Azad Hind Fauj) much later, in 1943.

questions.question: What was the main demand of the Home Rule Movement?
questions.option_4: Ban on all western education in India
questions.solution:
Objective of Home Rule

The primary demand of the Home Rule Movement was Self-governance within the British Empire. Unlike the later demand for 'Purna Swaraj' (Full Independence), the Home Rule leaders initially sought a 'Dominion Status' where Indians would manage internal affairs while remaining under the British Crown.

questions.question: The Red Shirt Movement, also known as Khudai Khidmatgar, was led by which Indian freedom fighter in the North-West Frontier Province?
questions.solution:
Red Shirt Movement (Khudai Khidmatgar)

Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan, also known as the 'Frontier Gandhi,' founded the Khudai Khidmatgar (Servants of God). Its members wore red uniforms, leading to the name 'Red Shirt Movement.' It was a non-violent movement that played a crucial role in the freedom struggle in the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP).

questions.question: What was the main reason for the protest against the Simon Commission in 1928?
questions.solution:
Simon Commission Protest

The Simon Commission (1928) was a group of seven British Members of Parliament sent to India to study constitutional reforms. It was boycotted by almost all Indian political parties because it had no Indian members. The slogan "Simon Go Back" became the rallying cry of the protest.

questions.question: The Swadeshi Movement, launched to oppose the partition of Bengal and promote Indian goods, began in which year?
questions.solution:
Swadeshi Movement (1905)

The Swadeshi Movement was a direct consequence of the Partition of Bengal by Lord Curzon in 1905. It emphasized the boycott of foreign (British) goods and the promotion of Indian-made (Swadeshi) products as a tool of political resistance.

questions.question: VO Chidambaram Pillai was actively involved in the revolutionary movement in which of the following areas?
questions.solution:
V.O. Chidambaram Pillai

V.O. Chidambaram Pillai (VOC) was a prominent leader from the Madras Presidency (specifically present-day Tamil Nadu). He is famous for establishing the Swadeshi Steam Navigation Company to challenge the British monopoly on shipping, earning him the title 'Kappalottiya Thamizhan' (The Tamil Helmsman).

questions.question: Sewa Singh Thikriwala was commemorated on his death anniversary on 20ᵗʰ January 2025. Which movement was he primarily associated with?
questions.solution:
Sewa Singh Thikriwala

Sewa Singh Thikriwala was a prominent leader of the Praja Mandal Movement in the Punjab princely states (specifically Patiala). The movement aimed at securing democratic rights for the people living in princely states and protested against the autocratic rule of the Kings (Maharajas) and British interference.

questions.question: Which prominent Indian leader led the Bardoli Satyagraha of 1928, a major civil disobedience movement against increased land taxes?
questions.solution:
Bardoli Satyagraha

The Bardoli Satyagraha of 1928 was a successful non-violent tax strike in Gujarat led by Vallabhbhai Patel. It was organized to protest a 22% increase in land revenue. Following the success of this movement, the women of Bardoli conferred the title 'Sardar' (Leader) upon him.

questions.question: In which year did Goa become the capital of the Portuguese Commercial empire in India?
questions.solution:
Goa as Portuguese Capital

Afonso de Albuquerque captured Goa in 1510, but it was in 1530 that Goa officially replaced Cochin as the capital of the Portuguese State of India (Estado da Índia) under Governor Nuno da Cunha.

questions.question: Which Act transferred the power of governance from the East India Company to the British Crown?
questions.solution:
Government of India Act, 1858

Following the Indian Rebellion of 1857, the British Parliament passed the Government of India Act, 1858. This act terminated the rule of the East India Company and transferred the administration of India directly to the British Crown (Queen Victoria). The Governor-General was given the title of Viceroy.

questions.question: Which of the following acts authorised the Governor General to override his council in matters affecting the interest of British empire in India?
questions.solution:
Charter Act of 1793

The Charter Act of 1793 officially confirmed the Governor-General’s power to override his council in extraordinary circumstances. This gave the executive head more authority to ensure the stability and interests of the British Empire in India during times of conflict or policy disputes.

questions.question: The Nizam of Hyderabad tried to project his rule as:
questions.solution:
The Nizam's Rule

The Nizam of Hyderabad, particularly the last Nizam, Mir Osman Ali Khan, attempted to project the Hyderabad state as a Muslim sovereignty in the Deccan. Despite having a Hindu-majority population, the administrative and military elite were predominantly Muslim, and the Nizam sought to maintain an independent Islamic identity for the state during the integration of India.

questions.question: Which of the following sessions of the INC passed a resolution opposing the Arms Act and the Vernacular Press Act?
questions.solution:
INC First Session (1885)

In the very first session of the Indian National Congress held in 1885 at Bombay, the leaders passed several resolutions. While the most famous protests against the Arms Act and Vernacular Press Act occurred during Lytton's era, the early Congress sessions consistently demanded the repeal of these repressive measures to ensure civil liberties.

questions.question: In the 1892 Indian Councils Act, the British government introduced certain reforms in response to Indian demands. Which of the following was a significant provision of this Act?
questions.option_1: Granting full self-governance to Indian provinces
questions.option_2: Establishment of a separate electorate for Muslims
questions.option_3: Provision for Indian representation in the Imperial Legislative Council
questions.option_4: Introduction of direct elections for legislative councils
questions.solution:
Indian Councils Act, 1892

The Indian Councils Act of 1892 was a response to the INC's demands for more say in governance. Its most significant provision was increasing the number of non-official members in the Imperial and Provincial Legislative Councils, effectively allowing for the first time a limited form of Indian representation, though members were still nominated rather than directly elected.
